summary: The author developed a coarse-to-fine pipeline for intracranial aneurysm detection that uses vessel segmentation to guide a region-of-interest (ROI) based classifier. Instead of a single end-to-end model, they first locate a coarse ROI using a low-resolution nnU-Net, then extract detailed vessel masks within that ROI using two high-resolution nnU-Net models. These masks are fed into a 3D classification model with a pre-trained nnU-Net backbone, location-aware transformer, and auxiliary sphere segmentation task to predict 13 anatomical locations and overall aneurysm presence. Key design principles include leveraging segmentation as a structural guide and using coarse-to-fine efficiency to balance accuracy and speed.
extensive_summary:
## Solution Overview & Pipeline
The solution employs a robust, coarse-to-fine pipeline designed to produce location-aware predictions for intracranial aneurysms. The process begins with DICOM preprocessing, where series are converted to NIfTI volumes, standardized in orientation, and normalized using per-volume z-score normalization. To ensure data quality, approximately 60 series with orientation anomalies, corruption, or implausible slice spacing were excluded.

## Vessel Segmentation & ROI Extraction
A critical component is the two-stage vessel segmentation process using three nnU-Net v2 models. A coarse, low-resolution model (1.0 mm spacing) first scans the entire volume to identify a candidate ROI. This is followed by two high-resolution models (0.80/0.45/0.44 mm spacing) that generate precise vessel masks within the cropped ROI. Model 2 uses Dice + Cross-Entropy + SkeletonRecall loss, while Model 3 prioritizes recall with Tversky + Cross-Entropy + SkeletonRecall (weight=3). Left-right mirroring was disabled to preserve anatomical asymmetry, and low-resolution simulation transforms were added for robustness.

## ROI Classification Architecture
The classification stage takes 128×256×256 voxel ROIs and uses a 3D model with a pre-trained nnU-Net backbone. The decoder was simplified by removing its final block for efficiency. To capture spatial relationships, the architecture implements a "Vessel Region-Masked Pooling" layer to extract per-location features, fuses them with global encoder features, and passes them through a "Location-Aware Transformer" before an MLP head predicts 13 location probabilities. An auxiliary task reconstructs a binary sphere at aneurysm locations to guide feature learning. The overall "Aneurysm Present" prediction pools features over the entire vessel structure.

## Training & Inference Details
Training utilized BCEWithLogitsLoss for classification tasks and a combination of Balanced BCE and Focal-Tversy++ loss for the auxiliary sphere task, with loss weights heavily favoring the auxiliary task (1.0) over classification (0.1 and 0.05). The model was trained with AdamW (lr=1e-4), gradient accumulation (batch size 8), cosine annealing with warmup, and EMA weights for inference. Final predictions were generated by averaging cross-validation fold outputs and applying test-time augmentation (TTA) via left-right flipping. A fail-safe mechanism falls back to pre-determined mean probabilities if segmentation fails.
competition_flow: Raw DICOM series are converted to standardized NIfTI volumes, processed through a coarse-to-fine nnU-Net vessel segmentation pipeline to extract ROIs and masks, and finally classified by a 3D transformer-augmented model to predict 13 anatomical locations and overall aneurysm presence.
data_reading: DICOM series converted to NIfTI volumes using dcm2niix (with gdcmconv --raw fallback), standardized to consistent anatomical orientation via nnU-Net’s SimpleITKIOWithReorient, and normalized with per-volume z-score.
data_processing: Excluded ~60 series with quality issues (orientation anomalies, corruption, implausible spacing).
Filtered slices to retain majority Rows × Cols and PixelSpacing, removing outlier interslice spacing.

Training augmentations included Gaussian noise/smoothing, intensity shift/scale, contrast adjustment, sharpening, inversion, random flips, ±10° rotations, ±10% scaling/shearing, grid distortions, and low-resolution simulation.
Left-right mirroring disabled for fine segmentation models.
ensembling: Final predictions are averaged across 4 of the 5 cross-validation folds, combined with test-time augmentation (averaging original and left-right flipped volume predictions), and include a fail-safe fallback to pre-determined mean probabilities if segmentation fails.
insights: Using explicit vessel segmentation masks as a structural guide significantly improves classification accuracy and training speed compared to end-to-end approaches.
Pretraining the classification backbone on the vessel segmentation task is the most critical factor for performance and convergence speed.
Treating each of the 14 labels as an independent binary classification problem effectively mitigates severe class imbalance for rare anatomical locations.
A coarse-to-fine inference strategy balances computational efficiency with high-resolution detail by restricting expensive high-resolution models to a candidate ROI.
critical_findings: Higher weights on classification losses led to overfitting, necessitating a heavy emphasis on the auxiliary sphere segmentation loss (weight=1.0) to stabilize training.
The Location-Aware Transformer and FocalTversy++ loss provided initial gains but ultimately contributed minimally to the final score compared to backbone pretraining and loss weighting.
An orientation correction method that perfectly fixed training set anomalies had zero measurable impact on the leaderboard score, indicating test data did not suffer from the same orientation issues.
what_did_not_work: A single end-to-end 3D classifier with auxiliary heads detected aneurysm presence but failed to accurately predict the 13 specific anatomical locations.
A simpler 2-channel input model (image + binary mask) requiring 14 separate forward passes per patient was computationally too expensive.

papers.title: nnU-Net: a self-configuring method for deep learning-based biomedical image segmentation
